name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
طسٓ ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ (١)
```

```english
Ta, Seen. These are the signs of the Quran and a manifest Book, (1)
```

```arabic
هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
```

```english
a guidance and good news for the faithful (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٣)
```

```english
—those who maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and who are certain of the Hereafter. (3)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (٤)
```

```english
As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made their deeds seem decorous to them, and so they are bewildered. (4)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ (٥)
```

```english
They are the ones for whom there is a terrible punishment, and they are the ones who will be the biggest losers in the Hereafter. (5)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٦)
```

```english
Indeed you receive the Quran from One who is all-wise, all-knowing. (6)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah—as indicated by its opening verses and the five concluding verses—is to provide glad tidings (التبشير) and warnings (الإنذار). To this end, it cites portions of the stories of Musa, Dawud, Sulayman, Salih, and Lut (peace be upon them). This is followed by an exposition of several fundamental principles of divine knowledge (أصول المعارف), such as the Oneness of Allah (وحدانيته تعالى) in Lordship (الربوبية), the Resurrection (المعاد), and other matters.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْقُرْآنِ وَكِتَابٍ مُبِينٍ** "These are the signs of the Quran and a manifest Book" (27:1). The demonstrative pronoun "these" (تِلْكَ)—as mentioned at the beginning of Surah al-Shu‘ara—refers to the verses of the Surah, both those yet to be revealed and those already revealed. The use of a term specifically for the distant (البعيد) is to indicate their exalted status (رفعة قدرها) and their being beyond easy reach (بعد منالها).

"The Quran" (القرآن) is a name for the Book (الكتاب) in consideration of its being recited (مقروا). "Manifest" (المبين) is derived from "clarification" (الإبانة), meaning to make something apparent (الإظهار). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "the Quran" (القرآن) is for magnification (التفخيم); that is: these verses of exalted status that We reveal are the verses of the Book and the verses of a recited Book of magnificent standing, which makes its objectives manifest (مبين) without ambiguity or complexity.

It is stated in *Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "He described it with two attributes—meaning 'the Book' and 'the Quran'—to convey that it is something that becomes manifest through recitation and manifest through writing; it is thus in the position of one who speaks through both matters. Describing it as 'manifest' (مبين) is a metaphor likening it to one who speaks clearly of such-and-such." [End of quote].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "a guidance and good news for the faithful" (27:2). The two verbal nouns (المصدران), namely "guidance" (هُدًى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and "good news" (بُشْرَى), carry the meaning of the active participle (اسم الفاعل), or they are intended in their original verbal noun sense for the sake of emphasis (المبالغة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلَاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ** "those who maintain the prayer and give the zakat" (27:3), etc. What is intended is the performance of righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة). He restricted the mention to prayer (الصلاة) and zakat (الزكاة) because each is a pillar in its respective domain: prayer regarding what pertains to Allah, the Exalted, and zakat regarding what pertains to the people. From another perspective, prayer is among the physical acts (الأعمال البدنية) and zakat is among the financial acts (الأعمال المالية).

His saying: **وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ** "and who are certain of the Hereafter" (27:3) is another description of the believers, coordinated (معطوف) with what preceded it. It is brought to indicate that these righteous deeds only find their proper place and achieve their purpose when accompanied by certainty (الإيقان) regarding the Hereafter; for indeed, deeds are nullified (يحبط) when the Hereafter is denied. The Exalted said: **وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَلِقَاءِ الْآخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ** "As for those who deny Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, their works have failed" (7:147).

The repetition of the pronoun in His saying: **وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ** (and they, of the Hereafter, they...) is to indicate that this certainty is their defining characteristic, and they are the people from whom such certainty is expected.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ** "As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made their deeds seem goodly to them, so they wander blindly" (27:4). **Blind wandering** (العمه) is to be bewildered (التحير) in an affair. The meaning of **making a deed seem goodly** (تزيين) is to make it such that a human being is attracted to it. Since those who do not believe in the Hereafter have denied it—though it is the ultimate destination of their journey—they remained in the world, which is a path (سبيل) and not a destination (غاية). Consequently, they became attached to their deeds therein and were bewildered on the path, having no goal toward which they might aim.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ** "It is they for whom there is a terrible punishment" (27:5), etc. This is a threat of absolute punishment, both worldly and in the Hereafter, as evidenced by His saying: **وَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ** "and they are the greatest losers in the Hereafter" (27:5). Perhaps the reason they are the greatest losers among people is that other sinners have records of deeds in which their evil deeds and good deeds are recorded and for which they are recompensed; as for these people, their evil deeds are preserved against them for recompense, while their good deeds are nullified (حابطة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى الْقُرْآنَ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ** "Indeed you receive the Quran from One who is all-wise, all-knowing" (27:6). "Receiving" (التلقية) is close in meaning to "instruction" (التلقين). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "All-Wise" (حَكِيمٍ) and "All-Knowing" (عَلِيمٍ) is for magnification (التعظيم). The explicit mention that this Quran is from His presence, the Exalted, is so that it may serve as a proof for the Messengerhood (الرسالة), a support for the previously mentioned divine knowledge, and a confirmation of the truth of the stories of the Prophets (peace be upon them) that He will soon mention.

The specific mention of these two Noble Names is to indicate that it descends from the fountainhead of wisdom (الحكمة)—thus no nullifier can abrogate it and no weakener can undermine it—and from the source of knowledge (العلم)—thus He does not lie in His reports nor err in His judgments.
